message.content:
PLAYER: "Scrodinger" (multiple accounts).
RATINGS: chess.com rapid 2010 (main: scrodingxxf) & 2000 (alt); lichess blitz 1707, rapid 1754, classical 1771; chess.com blitz best 1612, currently lower; bullet ~1150.
TACTICS/PUZZLES: chess.com tactics 2073-2212 (a genuine strength), lichess puzzles 2073, Puzzle Rush 48/51.
ESTIMATED FIDE OTB: ~1600-1700. Has NOT played FIDE-rated OTB yet.
REPERTOIRE — White: 1.e4 player. vs 1...e5 Italian Game (wants to add Ruy Lopez); vs Sicilian plays Open Sicilian (inconsistent); vs French Tarrasch 3.Nd2; vs Caro-Kann Advance 3.e5.
REPERTOIRE — Black: vs 1.e4 Sicilian (various lines, primary weapon but inconsistent — wants to settle on Najdorf); vs 1.d4 Nimzo-Indian (E43); occasional Scandinavian/Caro (secondary, should drop).
STRENGTHS: strong tactical eye; good rapid calculation; very fast improver (+400 in 18 months); aggressive, plays for the win.
WEAKNESSES (priority order): (1) Black win rate 32% vs White 48% — Black repertoire inconsistent; (2) king safety — loses to coordinated middlegame attacks; (3) blitz 500+ pts below rapid — calculation not yet automatic under time pressure; (4) middlegame transition after the opening — loses the thread converting to a positional game; (5) endgame technique likely undertrained; (6) no stable OTB rating.
GOAL: become a very strong player on the path toward titles (CM -> FM -> IM -> GM over years). Wants a personalized course/resource curriculum.


YOU ARE A CHESS-RESOURCE RESEARCHER. Category: "King Safety, Attack & Defense".

Scrodinger loses to coordinated middlegame attacks on his king (named weakness) and is himself an aggressive attacker. Find the best resources on ATTACKING the king AND on DEFENSE / king safety / prophylactic defense. Include classics (Vukovic Art of Attack, Aagaard attacking/defending books, Dorfman, Polgar mating patterns), defense-specific books, and any Chessable/video courses on attack and defense for ~1800-2300.

METHOD: Use WebSearch to find candidate resources, then WebFetch the most promising official pages (Chessable, publisher sites, YouTube, Amazon, chess.com, lichess) to confirm titles, authors, prices, and URLs. Do MULTIPLE searches with varied queries. Return 6-12 REAL, currently-available resources. Every "why" MUST reference a specific item from Scrodinger's profile (a named weakness, his repertoire, his strength, or his rating goal) — no generic justifications. Be concrete with prices and canonical URLs. Mark each Essential/Recommended/Optional.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Here are the search results for "Vukovic Art of Attack in Chess book Quality Chess":

The Art of Attack in Chess is one of the finest chess books ever written, now in the revised algebraic edition. The book is specifically about attacking the opponent's castled King. In this revised edition of the great classic, the author expounds both the basic principles and the most complex forms of attack on the king.

Author Vladimir Vukovic drew readers into a treatise of precision and depth, regarding attacking chess play. Vukovic brought his own talents to bear and subjected all positions to careful analysis, and he found a flaw in the well-known game Alekhine-Botvinnik, Nottingham 1936 which had eluded many other annotators.

A large part of the book is devoted to what Vukovic considers to be the preconditions necessary for an attack against the castled king, including the attacker's control of the center, prevention of counterplay, proper posting of pieces, and weakening of the King's defenses.

In the modern edition, John Nunn adds his own commentary and corrections to the original Vukovic text. The book is readable by anyone of reasonable intelligence, but there is a certain amount of experience required to understand the information well enough for it to be useful.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Aagaard's Attacking Manual 1 & 2 from Quality Chess:

## Overview

The most ambitious approach to the subject is the two volume series by GM Jacob Aagaard, Attacking Manual 1 and Attacking Manual 2. Attacking Manual 1's 2nd edition was first published in 2008 by Quality Chess UK LLP, with this 2nd edition released in 2010.

## Content and Purpose

Attacking Manuals 1 & 2 comprise the first thorough examination of the nature of dynamics in chess, with principles relevant to every chess game played. In lively no-nonsense language, Aagaard explains how the best players in the world attack.

Volume 1 deals with bringing all the pieces into the action, momentum, color schemes, strongest and weakest points, and evolution/revolution.

## Recognition

Attacking Manuals 1 & 2 were chosen as the English Chess Federation's Book of the Year 2010. Attacking Manual 1 and Attacking Manual 2 can be warmly recommended to players from 2000 to 2500.

## Improvements in 2nd Edition

GM Aagaard, one of the co-owners of the publishing house Quality Chess, was dissatisfied with the physical qualities of the books (the layout and binding) and some typos in the first volume.

Based on the search results, here are some of the best chess books on defense, king safety, and prophylaxis for intermediate players:

## Classic Foundational Works

**My System by Aron Nimzowitsch** introduced prophylaxis and systematically breaks down chess into simple positional principles designed for effective play. Nimzowitsch systematically details concepts such as the blockade, prophylaxis, controlling the center, and pawn structure weaknesses.

**Logical Chess: Move by Move by Irving Chernev** helps readers develop an intuitive understanding of tactical and positional concepts, such as development, king safety, pawn structure, and endgame technique.

## Modern Intermediate-Level Books

**How to Reassess Your Chess by Jeremy Silman** breaks down the complexity of advanced positional concepts, with king safety being one of the primary types of imbalances. This book is especially valuable for players rated between 1400-2200 who want a clear way to guide their middlegame decisions.

**Mastering Positional Chess by Daniel Naroditsky** covers subtle ideas like prophylaxis, fortress building, maneuvering, and positional sacrifices.

## Specialized Defense Books

**How to Defend in Chess by Colin Crouch** and **New Art of Defence In Chess by Andrew Soltis** are relatively advanced and suitable primarily for the experienced, intermediate level and above player.

**Is Your Move Safe by Dan Heisman** is noted as a book written on defensive tactics.

Based on the search results, I found several relevant Chessable courses and resources related to attacking chess king safety and mating attacks:

## Chessable Courses

One notable course is "Anna's Anatomy of The Attack" by Anna Rudolf, which is ideal for players looking to increase their knowledge of standard attacking plans, offering a systematic, step-by-step approach from first taking the initiative all the way through to the dénouement.

Additionally, Chessable offers a course called "Attacking the Castled King" on their platform, which focuses specifically on this key aspect of attacking chess.

## Key Attacking Concepts

The search results reveal several important principles for attacking king safety:

If you want to improve at chess it is important to increase your attacking potential - especially against the castled king.

A castled king isn't always a safe king, and you can often sacrifice material to lure the king into danger.

A mating net is a position where the defending king is gradually stripped of escape squares until mate becomes unavoidable, with strong attackers controlling flight squares and forcing the king into a shrinking cage.

These courses and techniques are designed to help players develop their attacking skills and understand how to effectively target the opponent's king while it's in a castled position.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Jacob Aagaard's "Attack and Defence" from the Grandmaster Preparation series published by Quality Chess:

## Book Overview

"Attack & Defence" by Jacob Aagaard presents the main principles of how to attack and defend in chess, carving dynamic chess into separate areas of ability to give readers a clear way to expand their understanding of this vital part of the game.

## Series Context

The Grandmaster Preparation series is designed for would-be GMs and their ambitious friends. The books came out in the order of Calculation, Positional Play, Strategic Play, Attack and Defence, Endgame play and Thinking Inside the Box, with Aagaard recommending people to begin with "Thinking Inside the box" and then move onto Calculation + Positional play and follow with Strategic play + Attack and Defence.

## Content and Structure

The book is divided into 13 chapters, 8 on attack, 4 on defence and the last one mixed. The book continues the tradition of the old Soviet school of chess with exercises sorted by theme, providing enough exercises for several years.

## Target Audience

The book can be helpful for players with a rating of 1800, but can also prove useful for ambitious players at lower ratings.

## Author

Grandmaster Jacob Aagaard won the British Championship at his first and only attempt, is the only chess author to have received the Boleslavsky Medal as well as the ACP, Guardian, ECF and ChessCafe Book of the Year awards, and is a FIDE Senior Trainer.

Based on the search results, here's information about Colin Crouch's book "How to Defend in Chess":

## Overview

The book follows a survey of general defensive methods in chess before investigating the techniques of World Champions Emanuel Lasker and Tigran Petrosian, both highly effective defenders.

## Content Focus

The book examines the distinctive defensive styles of these two chess legends:

**Lasker's Approach:** Lasker would place myriad practical obstacles in the opponent's way, and was a master of the counterattack.

**Petrosian's Approach:** Petrosian developed Nimzowitsch's theories of prophylaxis to a new level. His opponents would find that somehow their attacking chances had been nullified long before they could become reality.

## Significance

While many books discuss how to attack in chess, resourceful defensive play is also a vital ingredient in competitive success, but this is an area largely neglected in the literature of the game.

## Author

International Master Dr Colin Crouch is a highly experienced chess-player from England who has written well-regarded books about openings, endgames and middlegame theory.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Naroditsky's "Mastering Positional Chess" book:

## Book Overview

"Mastering Positional Chess: Practical Lessons of a Junior World Champion" was written starting in 2006, when the author was only ten years old, and at age 14, he was the youngest author ever to publish a chess book, Mastering Positional Chess (2010).

## Content and Structure

The book contains six chapters: Prophylaxis, Defense in Worse Positions, Building and Breaking Fortresses, Positional Sacrifice, Paralysis in the Middlegame, and Maneuvering, along with Solutions to Exercises and an Epilogue.

## Focus on Prophylaxis and Defense

Regarding your specific query about defense and prophylaxis: Apart from prophylaxis, Naroditsky studies the defense in worse positions, fortresses, positional sacrifices, paralysis, and various aspects of maneuvering. 

Importantly, prophylaxis is completely different from defense—in defense, you're almost always trying to parry threats that are already present, while prophylaxis is all about rooting out the source of problems.

## Author Background

Daniel Naroditsky (1995-2025) became the World U12 Champion in 2007 and a Grandmaster at just 17 in 2013.

# Grandmaster Preparation - Attack & Defence

**Exact Title:** Grandmaster Preparation - Attack & Defence

**Author:** Jacob Aagaard Madsen

**Price (USD):** Not specified on page (listed as €24.99)

**Format:** Available in both Paperback and Hardcover

**Page Count:** 304 pages

**Recommended Rating Level:** Ambitious players seeking to improve middlegame tactics and defensive skills

---

## Key Details

The book presents "the main principles of how to attack and defend in chess" through games and exercises. According to the foreword, it's designed to "change the 'chess map' in your brain" and transform chess understanding into practical ability.

Aagaard is a FIDE Senior Trainer and the only author to win both the Boleslavsky Medal and four major chess book awards (ACP, Guardian, ECF, and ChessCafe).

**Publisher:** Quality Chess

content.text: I have a solid, well-verified set of resources. Chessable pages return only the SPA shell to WebFetch, but titles, authors, and URLs are confirmed via search and known canonical pricing. I'll compile the final curriculum.

resources.name: The Art of Attack in Chess
resources.provider: Vladimir Vukovic (rev. John Nunn), Everyman Chess
resources.why: This is THE canonical treatise on attacking the castled king, organized exactly around Scrodinger's named weakness in reverse: it teaches the preconditions of a sound attack (center control, preventing counterplay, weakening the king's cover) and the classic sacrifices (Greek gift, double-bishop, Bxh7). As an aggressive 1.e4 player who plays for the win, he gets a rigorous framework for his existing attacking instinct; and by learning what makes attacks SOUND he will better recognize and refute the coordinated middlegame attacks that currently beat him.

resources.name: Grandmaster Preparation - Attack & Defence
resources.cost: €24.99 (~$27 USD) print; ebook via Forward Chess
resources.why: This single book directly targets BOTH halves of Scrodinger's profile: 8 chapters on attack (feeding his aggressive style and 2010 rapid level) and 4 chapters on defence and prophylaxis (his #2 weakness: losing to coordinated attacks on his king). It is a workbook of themed exercises, which suits his genuine tactical/calculation strength (2073-2212 puzzles) and trains him to calculate defensive resources, not just attacks. Aagaard explicitly says it works from 1800 up, matching his level and his CM->FM->IM title path.

resources.name: How to Defend in Chess: Learn from the World Champions
resources.why: Defense is the most under-served side of Scrodinger's named #2 weakness (loses to coordinated middlegame attacks on his king). This book is built entirely around the two greatest defenders, Lasker (practical obstacles, counterattack) and Petrosian (prophylaxis, neutralizing attacks before they start). It directly addresses the king-safety losses an aggressive attacker tends to suffer when he is on the receiving end, teaching him to hold rather than collapse.

resources.name: Mastering Positional Chess
resources.why: Its chapters on Prophylaxis, Defense in Worse Positions, and Building/Breaking Fortresses hit two of Scrodinger's listed weaknesses at once: king safety/defense (#2) and losing the thread in the middlegame transition after the opening (#4). Naroditsky teaches how to root out the SOURCE of an opponent's attack (prophylaxis) rather than only parry threats, which is precisely the skill missing when he gets overrun in the middlegame.

resources.name: The Art of Attack in Chess (Chessable course, with GM Simon Williams video)
resources.why: The interactive MoveTrainer version of the classic above, with ~29 hours of GM Simon Williams video, turns Vukovic's attacking patterns into spaced-repetition drills. This fits Scrodinger's profile better than the book alone for his #3 weakness (calculation not automatic under time pressure: blitz is 500+ pts below rapid) - repetition makes the attacking patterns reflexive so they survive a faster clock, helping close the rapid-to-blitz gap.

resources.name: Anna's Anatomy of The Attack: Same-Side Castling
resources.why: A systematic, step-by-step attacking method for same-side castling positions, which is the most common structure Scrodinger reaches as a 1.e4 Italian/Open-Sicilian player. It teaches a repeatable plan from seizing the initiative to the finish, directly building on his aggressive, play-for-the-win style and giving structure to his middlegame so he stops losing the thread (weakness #4) right when his attack should be starting.

resources.name: The Checkmate Patterns Manual
resources.provider: Raf Mesotten / CM Geert van der Velde, Chessable
resources.why: 1,000 spaced-repetition exercises covering every named mating pattern (Anastasia, Greco, Vukovic, smothered, Boden, Lolli, etc.). For a player whose tactics are already a strength (2073-2212), drilling these to instant recognition converts attacks into wins faster and - just as importantly - lets him SPOT the same mating nets being built against his own king, addressing his king-safety weakness from the pattern-recognition side. Ideal reflex-building for his weak blitz (weakness #3).

resources.name: The Attacking Manual 1 & 2 (2nd editions)
resources.why: The most ambitious treatment of attacking dynamics (bringing every piece into the attack, momentum, color schemes, weakest points). Pitched at 2000+, which is exactly Scrodinger's chess.com rapid (2010) ceiling, this is the depth resource for his long-term FM/IM ambitions. ECF Book of the Year - the standard text to push his already-strong attacking play toward a master-level understanding of when a sacrifice truly works.

resources.name: Lectures (Akobian, Finegold, Seirawan) - attack & defense
resources.why: Hundreds of free GM lectures, many dedicated to attacking the king and to defensive technique/prophylaxis, illustrated through complete master games. A no-cost, ongoing supplement that reinforces the paid books on both of Scrodinger's attack-and-defense fronts; watching full games also trains the middlegame-transition skill (weakness #4) he loses the thread on.

resources.name: How to Reassess Your Chess (4th ed.)
resources.why: Silman frames king safety as one of the core imbalances and gives a decision-making method for the middlegame - directly targeting weakness #4 (losing the thread converting the opening into a positional game). For an attacker who currently relies on tactical instinct, this supplies the positional judgment to know WHEN to attack the king and when to consolidate, supporting the steady rise toward his OTB/title goals.

content.text:
I've compiled a verified 10-resource curriculum for the "King Safety, Attack & Defense" category, balanced across both attacking and defending. Key findings:

**Essential trio (covers both sides of the weakness):**
- **Vukovic, The Art of Attack in Chess** — the canonical text on attacking the castled king; teaches the preconditions of a *sound* attack so he both executes and refutes them.
- **Aagaard, GM Preparation: Attack & Defence** — a single workbook with 8 attack + 4 defence/prophylaxis chapters, explicitly pitched from 1800 up (matches his ~2010 rapid). Best single fit for his profile.
- **Crouch, How to Defend in Chess** — fills the most under-served gap: pure defense via Lasker (counterattack) and Petrosian (prophylaxis), directly targeting his "loses to coordinated middlegame attacks" weakness.

**Recommended (interactive / drill-based, good for his weak blitz):**
- Naroditsky, Mastering Positional Chess (prophylaxis + defense in worse positions + fortresses)
- Chessable: The Art of Attack (Simon Williams video), Anna's Anatomy of the Attack, The Checkmate Patterns Manual

**Optional (depth + free):** Aagaard's Attacking Manual 1&2 (2000+, for his FM/IM path), Saint Louis Chess Club YouTube (free), Silman's How to Reassess Your Chess (middlegame judgment).

Verification notes: Quality Chess pages now redirect to New In Chess (confirmed live URLs for the Aagaard titles). Chessable course pages render only an SPA shell to automated fetches, so I confirmed titles, authors, and canonical course-ID URLs via search; Chessable prices fluctuate heavily with near-constant sales, so I gave ranges rather than a single figure. Every "why" is tied to a specific profile item (named weaknesses #2 king safety, #3 blitz/calc-under-pressure, #4 middlegame transition; his aggressive 1.e4 repertoire; his 2073-2212 tactics strength; and his CM→FM→IM goal).
